document_text:
Semantic Landmark Particle Filter for Robot Localisation in Vineyards
Rajitha de Silva1Jonathan Cox1James R. Heselden1Marija Popovi ´c2Cesar Cadena3Riccardo Polvara1
Abstract— Reliable localisation in vineyards is hindered
by row-level perceptual aliasing: parallel crop rows produce
nearly identical LiDAR observations, causing geometry-only
and vision-based SLAM systems to converge towards incorrect
corridors, particularly during headland transitions. We present
a Semantic Landmark Particle Filter (SLPF) that integrates
trunk and pole landmark detections with 2D LiDAR within a
probabilistic localisation framework. Detected trunks are con-
verted intosemantic walls, forming structural row boundaries
embedded in the measurement model to improve discrimination
between adjacent rows. GNSS is incorporated as a lightweight
prior that stabilises localisation when semantic observations are
sparse.
Field experiments in a 10-row vineyard demonstrate consis-
tent improvements over geometry-only (AMCL), vision-based
(RTAB-Map), and GNSS baselines. Compared to AMCL, SLPF
reduces Absolute Pose Error by 22% and 65% across two
traversal directions; relative to a NoisyGNSS baseline, APE
decreases by 65% and 61%. Row correctness improves from
0.67 to 0.73, while mean cross-track error decreases from
1.40 m to 1.26 m. These results show that embedding row-level
structural semantics within the measurement model enables
robust localisation in highly repetitive outdoor agricultural
environments.
I. INTRODUCTION
Accurate localisation is essential for long-term autonomy
in vineyards and orchards, where robots perform monitoring,
spraying, and yield estimation. However, vineyards consist of
highly repetitive parallel rows that induce severe perceptual
aliasing, making adjacent corridors geometrically indistin-
guishable for geometry-only localisation systems. Seasonal
vegetation changes further reduce robustness by introducing
transient structure while leaving only trunks and support
poles spatially stable.
LiDAR-based localisation remains widely used due to
its robustness to illumination changes, yet repetitive crop
geometry leads to persistent wrong-row hypotheses that
standard likelihood models struggle to reject [1], [2]. As
illustrated in Fig. 1, adjacent vineyard rows generate nearly
identical LiDAR signatures under geometry-only models.
Recent work has explored LiDAR-vision fusion to mitigate
these limitations. Vision-based trunk detection combined
with 2D LiDAR achieves accurate row following in orchards
[3], while trunk-aware graph-SLAM and keypoint-semantic
1Rajitha de Silva, Jonathan Cox, James R. Heselden and Ric-
cardo Polvara are with Lincoln Centre for Autonomous Systems (L-
CAS), University of Lincoln, UK.2Marija Popovi ´c is with MA VLab,
TU Delft, Netherlands.3Cesar Cadena is with Robotics Systems Lab,
ETH Zurich, Switzerland. (correspondence author: Rajitha de Silva
1odesilva@lincoln.ac.uk)
This work was supported by Engineering and Physical Sciences Research
Council (EPSRC), UK Project: “GAIA: Ground-Aerial maps Integration for
increased Autonomy outdoors” (EPSRC Reference: EP/Y003438/1).
Fig. 1: Vineyard semantics detection (left) projected onto the
landmark map (right) for likelihood calculation. Green: vine trunks,
Blue: support poles.
integration improve loop closure and feature distinctiveness
in vineyards [4], [5]. These studies demonstrate the value of
stable semantic landmarks.
However, most fusion approaches focus on reactive navi-
gation or optimisation-based SLAM. They do not explicitly
address row aliasing within a probabilistic global localisa-
tion framework. In particular, trunk detections are typically
treated as isolated landmarks rather than as structural con-
straints capable of disambiguating parallel corridors.
We propose a Semantic Landmark Particle Filter (SLPF)
that integrates RGB-D trunk and pole detections with 2D
LiDAR observations in a probabilistic formulation. The key
idea is to convert stable landmarks intosemantic walls:
row-level structural boundaries inferred from the spatial
arrangement of trunks and support poles. Instead of treating
each detection independently, neighbouring landmarks are
interpreted as belonging to the same physical row, forming
continuous corridor constraints that reflect the underlying
vineyard layout.
These semantic walls are embedded directly within the
particle-filter likelihood, strengthening discrimination be-
tween adjacent rows and explicitly penalising cross-row
hypotheses under perceptual aliasing. GNSS is included as
a lightweight probabilistic prior that primarily stabilises lo-
calisation in headlands where semantic observations become
sparse.
The contributions of this paper are threefold. First,
we introduce semantic walls, transforming sparse trunk
and pole detections into row-aligned structural constraints
that improve row-level discrimination. Second, we embed
these constraints directly within a semantic-LiDAR par-
ticle filter, enabling explicit rejection of wrong-row hy-
potheses in repetitive environments. Third, we incorpo-
rate adaptive GNSS weighting based on semantic observa-arXiv:2603.10847v1  [cs.RO]  11 Mar 2026

tion richness, improving robustness during headland tran-
sitions while preserving lightweight sensor requirements.
Real-world experiments demonstrate improved row cor-
rectness and recovery compared to geometry-only and
visual-SLAM baselines. Code and dataset available at:
https://github.com/LCAS/Outdoor SLPF.
II. RELATEDWORK
Robot localisation in vineyards presents several challenges
due to the environment’s repetitive row structure, seasonal
appearance changes, and uneven terrain conditions [1], [2].
Visual sensors such as LiDAR and cameras are prone to per-
ceptual aliasing in repetitive environments and are sensitive
to lighting variation [6]. Non-visual sensors, including GNSS
and ultrasonic systems, can be affected by canopy occlusion,
multipath, and signal degradation [7], [8]. Robust localisa-
tion remains essential for enabling long-term autonomy and
digital twin applications in vineyard management [9], [10].
LiDAR-based particle filters such as Adaptive Monte
Carlo Localization (AMCL) [11] have been widely applied
in structured environments and extended to agricultural do-
mains [12], [13]. While their probabilistic formulation allows
tolerance to map-sensor mismatch [14], their measurement
models rely purely on geometric consistency. In vineyards,
where parallel rows generate near-identical range signatures,
geometry-only likelihoods may maintain persistent wrong-
row hypotheses, particularly during headland transitions.
Visual SLAM approaches have also been explored in
vineyards [4], [15]. Papadimitriou et al. [4] incorporate trunk
detections into a graph-SLAM formulation, demonstrating
improved loop closure robustness compared to non-semantic
baselines. Similarly, De Silva et al. [5] introduce keypoint-
semantic integration to improve visual feature distinctiveness
in repetitive agricultural environments. These works high-
light the value of semantically stable landmarks such as
vine trunks, yet they remain embedded within optimisation-
based SLAM back-ends rather than explicitly reshaping the
measurement likelihood in a global probabilistic filter.
Several recent works explore LiDAR-vision fusion for row
navigation. For example, Shi et al. [3] combine YOLO-
based trunk detection with 2D LiDAR via ray projection
to extract navigation lines in dense orchard environments,
achieving high lateral accuracy. Such approaches effectively
support corridor following but assume locally continuous
vegetation and are primarily designed for reactive control
rather than maintaining a global pose posterior. Stable-point
segmentation methods further distinguish permanent struc-
tural elements from seasonal vegetation to enhance long-term
consistency in 3D LiDAR mapping [16]. While improving
robustness to appearance changes, these approaches typically
operate within dense SLAM frameworks and do not directly
address row-level aliasing in global localisation.
GNSS-based localisation remains common in agricultural
robotics [17]. Sensor-fusion approaches combining SLAM
and GNSS dynamically adjust weighting based on signal
quality metrics [18]. While effective in GNSS-degradedscenarios, such methods often assume RTK precision or rely
on factor-graph optimisation.
In contrast to prior work, our approach makes three
conceptual shifts. First, rather than treating trunk detections
as isolated landmarks, we connect adjacent stable landmarks
into piecewise-linear semantic walls, forming row-aligned
structural constraints that persist even when individual plants
are missing. Second, these semantic walls are embedded
directly within the particle-filter measurement likelihood,
enabling explicit penalisation of cross-row hypotheses under
perceptual aliasing. Third, GNSS is incorporated as an adap-
tive probabilistic prior whose influence depends on semantic
observation richness, providing global anchoring without
dominating the estimation process.
Together, these design choices specifically address the
row-aliasing failure mode characteristic of repetitive agricul-
tural environments. More broadly, they illustrate how seman-
tically stable, spatially organised landmarks can be converted
into structural constraints directly within a probabilistic
measurement model. While demonstrated in vineyards, this
formulation is applicable to other structured outdoor domains
exhibiting parallel or repetitive geometry, such as orchards,
forestry corridors, or plantation fields.
III. SEMANTICPARTICLEFILTERLOCALISATION IN
VINEYARDS
Vineyards exhibit strong perceptual aliasing: adjacent rows
share repeated geometry, making geometry-only localisation
prone to convergence to incorrect corridors when obser-
vations are sparse or ambiguous. We therefore formulate
localisation as asemantic landmark particle filter(SLPF),
a recursive Bayesian estimator that maintains multiple pose
hypotheses and evaluates them using motion and semanti-
cally informed measurement models.
Our approach leverages long-term stable vineyard land-
marks, such as vine trunks and support poles, detected via
instance segmentation and projected into a local bird’s-eye
view (BEV). These landmarks are used to semantically label
near-field LiDAR returns, producing class-conditioned obser-
vations. Observations are evaluated against a surveyed row-
aligned structural map derived from RTK-GNSS landmarks
(Fig. 2), while a NoisyGNSS prior provides a soft global
anchor when semantic evidence is weak (e.g., in headlands).
Fig. 3 summarises the pipeline.
A. Semantic Landmark Detection and BEV Projection
We focus on two long-term stable landmark classes:sup-
port polesandvine trunks. A YOLOv9-based instance seg-
mentation model is trained on the SemanticBLT dataset [19],
[20]. The segmentation module is modular and can be
replaced without modifying the downstream particle-filter
formulation.
For each detected instanceiwith maskM i, its range is
estimated conservatively using the closest valid depth:
di= min{D(p)|p∈M i, D(p)>0}(1)

B. Row-aligned Structural Map
Adjacent landmarks within the same row are connected
into line segments, referred to as semantic walls. Unlike
dense map representations, semantic walls encode only stable
row boundaries (RTK-GNSS-surveyed trunks and poles)
and intentionally ignore transient vegetation structure. These
walls encode row continuity explicitly and provide stronger
geometric discrimination between neighbouring corridors
during ray casting.
Landmarks are grouped by row ID and sorted along
the dominant axis; consecutive landmarks{a, b}define a
segment ab. A segment class follows a conservative pole-

augmented with a NoisyGNSS prior.

Resampling is triggered whenN efffalls below a threshold;

The pose estimate is computed via weighted mean for

particle when circular variance is high.G. Computational Considerations
The dominant cost is ray–segment intersection, scaling
asO(N pJtNs). Background rays are downsampled and
batched intersection with segment chunking is used to main-
tain tractable runtime.
IV. EXPERIMENTALEVALUATION
We evaluate SLPF in a controlled vineyard environment
to assess its ability to resolve row-level perceptual aliasing
under realistic sensing conditions.
A. Experimental Setup
Experiments were conducted using a Thorvald robot plat-
form (Saga Robotics) equipped with a front-mounted Intel
RealSense D435i RGB-D camera, a 2D LiDAR (SICK
Tim7), and an RTK-GNSS receiver. The robot traversed a
compact test vineyard consisting of 10 rows, each approx-
imately 16.86 m long. The vineyard covers about 385 m2
(0.0385 ha), corresponding to an approximate footprint of
17.0 m×22.6 m with a mean row spacing of∼2.50 m. Dur-
ing the trials, synchronised RGB-D images, LiDAR scans,
and RTK ground-truth poses were recorded.
To emulate realistic noisy GNSS conditions, the RTK
trajectory was degraded using a stochastic noise model
capturing three common error modes: (i) slowly varying
drift (temporally correlated bias), (ii) short-term receiver
noise (zero-mean Gaussian), and (iii) occasional jump-like
multipath errors (heavy-tailed outliers) [21], [22], [23]. We
report results over three independent noise realisations while
preserving original timestamps and orientations.
Evaluation is performed on two traversals using the same
semantic-wall map (see Fig. 2). For stochastic compo-
nents, three random seeds ({11,22,33}) are used and statis-
tics are aggregated across seeds; representative trajectory
plots use seed 11. We compare SLPF against NoisyGNSS,
AMCL, AMCL+NoisyGNSS (Kalman fusion), and RTAB-
Map (RGB and RGBD) [24]. AMCL is run with default
parameters, except that LiDAR maximum range is trun-
cated to5m to match the semantic sensing range used
by SLPF, ensuring comparable observation horizons. RTAB-
Map (RGB/RGBD) uses default configuration files.
SLPF parameters are kept fixed across both traversals
to avoid environment-specific tuning. Key settings include
miss/wrong-hit penalties (4.0/4.0), GNSS weight 0.5 with
adaptive scalingK= 4, semantic noiseσ sem= 0.05, GNSS
noiseσ gnss = 1.1m, corridor weight 0.30, background
weight 0.20, particle countN p= 100, frame stride 4, and
maximum semantic ranger max= 5m. Pose smoothing and
yaw filtering parameters are fixed across runs, and CUDA
acceleration is enabled without visualisation.
Performance is evaluated using raw and aligned Absolute
Pose Error (APE), Relative Pose Error (RPE at 2 m and 5 m
segments), cross-track error (XT), row-correct fraction, and
row mislocalisation events.

B. Results and Discussion
Across both traversal directions, SLPF consistently
achieves the lowest raw APE among all methods. In Experi-
ment 1, SLPF attains1.07±0.09m, improving over AMCL
(1.37±0.47m) and AMCL+NoisyGNSS (1.33±0.46m), and
substantially outperforming NoisyGNSS (3.04±0.13m). In
Experiment 2, SLPF remains stable at1.24±0.04m, whereas
AMCL and AMCL+NoisyGNSS degrade to3.50±0.94m
and3.38±0.91m, respectively. Overall, SLPF reduces APE
by 22% (Experiment 1) and 65% (Experiment 2) relative to
AMCL, and by 65% and 61% relative to NoisyGNSS.
These improvements translate directly to row-level met-
rics. SLPF attains the highest row-correct fraction in both
experiments (0.73±0.01and0.67±0.02), exceeding AMCL-
based baselines and RTAB-Map variants. Mean cross-track
error is also lowest for SLPF (1.26±0.06m and1.46±
0.03m), indicating tighter adherence to the correct corridor.Fig. 4 illustrates the qualitative behaviour. AMCL and
AMCL+NoisyGNSS produce smooth trajectories but remain
vulnerable to geometric aliasing: once drifting into an ad-
jacent row, recovery is limited. SLPF mitigates this failure
mode through semantic wall constraints and adaptive GNSS
weighting, enabling recovery from wrong-row hypotheses.
NoisyGNSS remains globally bounded but lacks structural
information, resulting in lower row correctness. RTAB-Map
achieves low short-horizon RPE, consistent with locally
smooth tracking; however, its large raw APE and reduced
row-correct fraction indicate global row misalignment, par-
ticularly during headland transitions where visual similarity
is amplified. Using SE(3)-aligned APE, the RMSE decreases
to approximately 10 m, confirming locally consistent tracking
but persistent global row misalignment in the surveyed map
frame. Default RTAB-Map configurations are retained to
reflect typical deployment rather than environment-specific

Row-mislocalisation events should be interpreted jointly
with row-correct and cross-track metrics, as they count tran-
sitions rather than duration. Although AMCL-based methods
report fewer switch events in Experiment 1, SLPF achieves
higher row correctness and lower cross-track error, indicating
shorter and recoverable wrong-row episodes. Importantly,
ablation results (Table III) show that removing the corridor
term degrades performance but does not eliminate the gains
from semantic likelihood integration, indicating that the pri-
mary improvement arises from structural semantic modelling
rather than corridor regularisation alone.
a) Failure Mode Analysis:Row-level aliasing is most
pronounced during headland transitions, where rapid heading
changes and reduced landmark visibility increase ambiguity
between adjacent corridors. In these conditions, geometry-
only filters may converge to a neighbouring row that pro-
duces locally consistent LiDAR ranges. Because parallel
rows yield similar ray signatures, such hypotheses can per-
sist, resulting in smooth trajectories with low short-horizon
RPE but large raw APE and reduced row correctness (see
Fig. 5). SLPF mitigates this behaviour through structural
discrimination. When semantic observations reappear, the
wall-based likelihood penalises cross-row inconsistencies,
causing incorrect particle clusters to collapse. The adaptive
GNSS prior stabilises pose during low-semantic intervals
without overwhelming corridor constraints.
These results indicate that SLPF directly addresses the
aliasing-induced wrong-row failure mode rather than merely
reducing average localisation error.
b) Performance Analysis:On our test workstation

ms/frame), while particle-filter likelihood evaluation and re-
sampling require approximately 8 ms/frame, supporting real-
time execution in the tested configuration, with particle-filter
evaluation accounting for a small fraction of total runtime.
c) Summary:Overall, Table I and Fig. 4 demonstrate
that SLPF provides improved global consistency and corridor
adherence across traversal directions. These results suggest
that the proposed semantic-likelihood formulation is particu-
larly beneficial under increased perceptual ambiguity, rather
than being specific to a given traversal configuration.
C. Robustness to Detection and Map Degradation
We evaluate two stress tests on Experiment 1:(A)random
semantic-detection dropping (20%, 40%), referred to as
Detection Dropout (DetDrop), and(B)landmark removal
from one contiguous map section (30%, 50%), referred to as
Section Landmark Removal (MapRemove). Table II follows
the same metrics/protocol used in the main comparison and
reports mean±std over three seeds.
For Detection Dropout (DetDrop), performance remains
stable under increasing semantic sparsity. APE increases
modestly from1.00±0.03m (baseline) to1.02±0.08m
(20%) and1.05±0.09m (40%), while row correctness
remains comparable or slightly improves (0.72±0.02to
0.73±0.01and0.74±0.03). The number of row mislocalisa-
tion events remains within variance, indicating that moderate
landmark loss does not destabilise the filter.
For Section Landmark Removal (MapRemove), perfor-
mance degrades progressively with increasing map sparsity.
Global APE rises from1.00±0.03m to1.05±0.04m (30%)
and1.13±0.13m (50%), while row correctness remains
stable at0.72±0.01. Although landmark removal locally
perturbs estimation within the affected section, the system
consistently recovers once re-entering mapped regions, with
recovery observed in all runs and an average recovery
distance of approximately 12 m.
D. Ablation Study
Table III reports the principal ablations of SLPF on Exper-
iment 1. The full configuration provides the most balanced
performance across global accuracy, corridor adherence, and
row consistency.

TABLE III: SLPF ablation study (mean±std across 3 seeds).
VariantAPE RMSE↓[m] RPE RMSE↓[m] Cross-track↓[m] Row correct↑Row mislocalisation↓
Raw Align 2 m 5 m Mean Fraction # eventsExperiment 1nonwall points0.98±0.08 0.95±0.073.34±0.07 6.91±0.06 1.30±0.03 0.70±0.03 32.67±2.49
static gnss weight 1.16±0.12 1.13±0.11 3.38±0.06 6.94±0.17 1.26±0.040.74±0.0331.33±1.70
nopose smoothing 1.11±0.01 1.08±0.003.19±0.03 6.55±0.09 1.24±0.000.71±0.01 40.67±0.47
poles only 1.15±0.20 1.15±0.20 3.37±0.06 7.00±0.21 1.29±0.06 0.69±0.03 32.33±1.25
trunks only 1.04±0.14 1.04±0.14 3.35±0.10 6.96±0.11 1.29±0.05 0.72±0.02 33.33±1.25
nobackground 1.44±0.53 1.31±0.42 3.30±0.05 6.63±0.11 1.43±0.06 0.67±0.03 33.67±1.25
nocorridor 1.37±0.12 1.34±0.12 3.28±0.03 6.81±0.13 1.44±0.02 0.67±0.02 35.33±2.05
nosemantic 1.64±0.64 1.44±0.48 3.31±0.11 6.91±0.01 1.50±0.20 0.67±0.02 33.00±0.00
nognss 7.55±4.87 4.28±1.03 3.26±0.11 6.65±0.32 3.18±0.84 0.32±0.0820.00±5.10
full (SLPF)1.07±0.09 1.04±0.10 3.33±0.07 6.92±0.09 1.26±0.06 0.73±0.01 34.67±1.70
a) Semantic Walls vs. Point-based Matching:The
nonwall pointsvariant replaces semantic walls with
point-to-point semantic matching. While it attains slightly
lower raw APE, row correctness decreases relative to the full
method. This indicates that local geometric fitting alone is
insufficient under perceptual aliasing, whereas encoding row
continuity through semantic walls improves discrimination
between adjacent corridors.
b) Poles-only vs. Trunks-only Semantics:The
poles onlyandtrunks onlyvariants isolate each
semantic class. Trunks-only achieves higher row correctness
(0.72) than poles-only (0.69), suggesting that trunks provide
denser and more consistently observable row-aligned
cues. However, both underperform the full configuration,
demonstrating that combining semantic classes yields a
more stable likelihood across varying visibility conditions.
c) Role of the GNSS Prior:Removing GNSS
(nognss) leads to substantial degradation in APE, cross-
track error, and row correctness, confirming that the GNSS
prior is critical for maintaining global consistency when
semantic evidence weakens (e.g., headlands). The reduced
number of row-switch events reflects prolonged persistence
in an incorrect row rather than improved stability. Using
a fixed GNSS weight (static gnss weight) slightly
increases row correctness but worsens APE, supporting the
use of dynamic weighting to balance local semantic evidence
with global priors.
d) Pose Smoothing:Disabling pose smoothing
(nopose smoothing) marginally improves short-
horizon RPE but increases row mislocalisation events. This
suggests that smoothing suppresses transient row-to-row
oscillations even if it slightly penalises short-term relative
accuracy.
e) Background and Corridor Terms:Removing
the background or corridor terms (no background,
nocorridor) degrades APE, cross-track error, and row
correctness. These components therefore act as structural
regularisers: background consistency constrains free-space
interpretation, while the corridor term biases particles
toward feasible inter-row regions, improving robustness
under repetitive geometry.f) Semantic Likelihood:Finally, removing semantic
likelihood cues entirely (no semantic) degrades all met-
rics relative to the full system, demonstrating that semantic
information is a primary contributor to SLPF performance
beyond the GNSS prior alone.
E. Limitations
The proposed formulation assumes the availability of a
surveyed semantic-wall map and reliable detection of stable
landmarks. Severe occlusion, structural reconfiguration, or
prolonged degradation of both semantic and GNSS signals
may reduce global consistency. While robustness experi-
ments demonstrate graceful degradation, future work will
investigate dynamic map updating and cross-season adap-
tation.
V. CONCLUSION
We presented a Semantic Landmark Particle Filter (SLPF)
for robust localisation in vineyards affected by row-level
perceptual aliasing. By detecting stable landmarks (trunks
and poles) and organising them into row-aligned structural
constraints, the method embeds corridor topology directly
into the particle-filter likelihood, enabling explicit rejec-
tion of cross-row hypotheses in repetitive environments. A
lightweight GNSS prior further stabilises localisation during
headland transitions where semantic cues are sparse.
Across two traversal directions, SLPF consistently im-
proves global map-frame accuracy and corridor adherence
relative to geometry-only and GNSS-based baselines. Com-
pared to AMCL, raw Absolute Pose Error is reduced by 22%
and 65%; relative to a NoisyGNSS baseline, reductions of
65% and 61% are observed. Improvements in row correct-
ness and cross-track error confirm more reliable recovery
from wrong-row hypotheses while maintaining stable row
tracking.
Future work will investigate cross-seasonal robustness
through multi-season trials and domain adaptation for the
panoptic segmentation model. We also aim to extend the
formulation to other structured agricultural environments,
such as orchards and forestry, where semantically organised
landmark constraints may similarly enhance long-term local-
isation.

structure.title: Semantic Landmark Particle Filter for Robot Localisation in Vineyards
structure.summary: The partial document discusses a novel approach to robot localization in vineyards using a Semantic Landmark Particle Filter (SLPF). The main points covered include the challenges of perceptual aliasing in vineyards, where parallel crop rows produce nearly identical LiDAR observations, causing geometry-only and vision-based SLAM systems to converge towards incorrect corridors. The proposed SLPF integrates trunk and pole landmark detections with 2D LiDAR within a probabilistic localization framework, converting detected trunks into semantic walls that form structural row boundaries to improve discrimination between adjacent rows. The document also highlights the incorporation of GNSS as a lightweight prior to stabilize localization when semantic observations are sparse. Field experiments demonstrate consistent improvements over geometry-only, vision-based, and GNSS baselines, with reduced Absolute Pose Error and improved row correctness. The key contributions of the paper include the introduction of semantic walls, embedding these constraints within a semantic-LiDAR particle filter, and incorporating adaptive GNSS weighting based on semantic observations.

nodes.title: I. INTRODUCTION
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses a Semantic Landmark Particle Filter (SLPF) for robot localization in vineyards. The main points covered include the challenges of reliable localization in vineyards due to row-level perceptual aliasing, the introduction of semantic walls to improve row-level discrimination, and the embedding of these constraints within a particle-filter likelihood. The document also highlights the incorporation of GNSS as a lightweight probabilistic prior to stabilize localization in headlands where semantic observations become sparse. The approach is compared to geometry-only and vision-based SLAM systems, demonstrating consistent improvements in localization accuracy and row correctness. The key contributions of the paper include the introduction of semantic walls, the embedding of these constraints within a semantic-LiDAR particle filter, and the incorporation of adaptive GNSS weighting based on semantic observation richness.

nodes.title: II. RELATED WORK
nodes.summary:
The partial document discusses a novel approach to robot localization in vineyards, which is a challenging task due to the repetitive row structure, seasonal appearance changes, and uneven terrain conditions. The main points covered include: 

1. Introduction of a semantic particle filter (SLPF) that leverages long-term stable vineyard landmarks, such as vine trunks and support poles, to improve localization accuracy.
2. Discussion of related work, including LiDAR-based particle filters, visual SLAM approaches, and GNSS-based localization methods, highlighting their limitations in addressing row-aliasing in repetitive agricultural environments.
3. Description of the SLPF approach, which connects adjacent stable landmarks into piecewise-linear semantic walls, forming row-aligned structural constraints that persist even when individual plants are missing.
4. Explanation of the semantic landmark detection and BEV projection process, using a YOLOv9-based instance segmentation model to detect support poles and vine trunks.
5. Overview of the particle filter formulation, including motion model, ray casting prediction, and likelihood estimation, which integrates semantic rays, background free-space constraints, GNSS anchoring, and a structural corridor prior.
6. Discussion of the benefits of the SLPF approach, including improved row correctness and recovery compared to geometry-only and visual-SLAM baselines, and its applicability to other structured outdoor domains exhibiting parallel or repetitive geometry.

nodes.title: III. SEMANTIC PARTICLE FILTER LOCALISATION IN VINEYARDS
nodes.summary: The partial document describes a vineyard mapping system using a combination of RTK-GNSS ground-truth traverses, camera intrinsics, and LiDAR points. The main points covered include the creation of a semantic wall map, which encodes row boundaries and ignores transient vegetation structure, and a particle filter formulation for localization. The particle filter uses a motion model, ray casting prediction, and likelihood estimation to integrate semantic rays, background free-space constraints, GNSS anchoring, and a structural corridor prior. The system also involves back-projecting representative pixels into the camera frame, transforming them into the robot frame, and retaining ground-plane coordinates. Additionally, the document mentions the use of instance masks from segmentation, depth, and BEV projections to generate semantic observations for the particle filter.

nodes.title: A. Semantic Landmark Detection and BEV Projection
nodes.summary: The partial document describes a vineyard mapping system using a combination of RTK-GNSS ground-truth traverses, camera intrinsics, and LiDAR points. The main points covered include the creation of a semantic wall map, which encodes row boundaries and ignores transient vegetation structure, and a particle filter formulation for localization. The particle filter uses a motion model, ray casting prediction, and likelihood estimation to integrate semantic rays, background free-space constraints, GNSS anchoring, and a structural corridor prior. The system also involves back-projecting representative pixels into the camera frame, transforming them into the robot frame, and retaining ground-plane coordinates. Additionally, the document discusses the grouping of observations into semantic sets, the connection of adjacent landmarks into line segments, and the use of a conservative pole-dominance rule for segment classification.

nodes.title: B. Row-aligned Structural Map
nodes.summary: The partial document describes a vineyard mapping system using a combination of LiDAR, camera, and GNSS data. The main points covered include the creation of a semantic structural map of the vineyard, which encodes row boundaries and landmarks such as poles and trunks. The document also outlines a particle filter formulation for estimating the state of a robot navigating the vineyard, using a motion model, ray casting prediction, and likelihood estimation based on semantic and background rays, as well as GNSS anchoring and a structural corridor prior. The system aims to provide a robust and accurate mapping of the vineyard, ignoring transient vegetation structure and focusing on stable row boundaries.

nodes.title: C. Particle Filter Formulation
nodes.summary: The partial document describes a vineyard mapping system using a combination of LiDAR, camera, and GNSS data. The main points covered include the creation of a semantic structural map of the vineyard, which encodes row boundaries and ignores transient vegetation structure. The document also outlines a particle filter formulation for estimating the state of a robot navigating the vineyard, using a motion model, ray casting prediction, and likelihood estimation based on semantic rays, background free-space constraints, GNSS anchoring, and a structural corridor prior. Additionally, the document explains how landmarks are inflated to circular regions, projected onto the ground plane, and labeled with semantic classes such as pole, trunk, and background. The system aims to provide a robust and efficient way to map and navigate vineyards.

nodes.title: 1) Motion Model
nodes.summary: The partial document describes a vineyard mapping system using a combination of RTK-GNSS ground-truth traverses, camera intrinsics, and LiDAR points. The main points covered include the creation of a semantic wall map, which encodes row boundaries and ignores transient vegetation structure, and a particle filter formulation that uses semantic observations, motion models, and likelihood estimation to localize the robot. The document also discusses the use of ray casting prediction, semantic and background rays, and a structural corridor prior to estimate the likelihood of the robot's position. Additionally, it mentions the use of instance masks, depth, and BEV projections to generate semantic observations and the application of a noisy GNSS prior to augment the particle filter. The overall goal of the system is to provide a robust and accurate method for mapping and localizing a robot in a vineyard environment.

nodes.title: 2) Ray Casting Prediction
nodes.summary: The partial document describes a vineyard mapping system using a combination of LiDAR, camera, and GNSS data. The main points covered include the creation of a semantic structural map of the vineyard, which encodes row boundaries and ignores transient vegetation structure. The document also outlines a particle filter formulation for estimating the state of a robot navigating the vineyard, using a motion model, ray casting prediction, and likelihood estimation that integrates semantic rays, background free-space constraints, GNSS anchoring, and a structural corridor prior. Additionally, the document explains how landmarks are inflated to circular regions, projected onto the ground plane, and labeled with semantic classes such as pole, trunk, and background. The system uses a conservative pole-dominance rule to classify segments and encode row continuity explicitly.

nodes.title: D. Likelihood Estimation
nodes.summary: The partial document describes a vineyard mapping system using a combination of RTK-GNSS ground-truth traverses, camera intrinsics, and LiDAR points. The main points covered include the creation of a semantic wall map, which encodes row boundaries and ignores transient vegetation structure, and a particle filter formulation for localization. The particle filter uses a motion model, ray casting prediction, and likelihood estimation to integrate semantic rays, background free-space constraints, GNSS anchoring, and a structural corridor prior. The system also involves back-projecting representative pixels into the camera frame, transforming them into the robot frame, and retaining ground-plane coordinates. Additionally, the document mentions the use of instance masks from segmentation, depth, and BEV projections to generate semantic observations for the particle filter.

nodes.title: 1) Semantic and Background Rays
nodes.summary: The partial document describes a vineyard mapping system using a combination of LiDAR, camera, and GNSS data. The main points covered include the creation of a semantic structural map of the vineyard, which encodes row boundaries and landmarks such as poles and trunks. The document also outlines a particle filter formulation for estimating the state of a robot navigating the vineyard, using a motion model, ray casting prediction, and likelihood estimation that incorporates semantic rays, background free-space constraints, GNSS anchoring, and a structural corridor prior. The system uses a combination of computer vision and sensor fusion techniques to generate a map of the vineyard and estimate the robot's position within it.

nodes.title: 2) GNSS Prior
nodes.summary: The partial document describes a vineyard mapping system using a combination of RTK-GNSS ground-truth traverses, camera intrinsics, and LiDAR points. The main points covered include the creation of a semantic wall map, which encodes row boundaries and ignores transient vegetation structure, and a particle filter formulation that uses semantic observations and a noisy GNSS prior to estimate the likelihood of the system's state. The document also discusses the use of ray casting prediction, likelihood estimation, and the integration of semantic rays, background free-space constraints, and GNSS anchoring to improve the accuracy of the system. Additionally, it covers the formulation of a motion model, ray casting prediction, and likelihood estimation, which are used to update the particle filter and estimate the system's state. The overall goal of the system is to create a robust and accurate vineyard mapping system that can be used for various applications such as autonomous navigation and crop monitoring.

nodes.title: 3) Corridor Prior
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses a Semantic Particle Filter (SLPF) for robot localization in a vineyard environment. The main points covered include the formulation of the SLPF, which integrates semantic observations from LiDAR and camera data, GNSS prior, and a structural corridor prior to estimate the robot's pose. The filter uses a particle-based approach, where each particle represents a possible robot pose, and the likelihood of each particle is evaluated based on the semantic observations, GNSS measurements, and corridor constraints. The document also describes the experimental evaluation of the SLPF in a controlled vineyard environment, where the filter is compared to other localization methods, including NoisyGNSS, AMCL, and RTAB-Map. The evaluation metrics include Absolute Pose Error, Relative Pose Error, cross-track error, row-correct fraction, and row mislocalization events.

nodes.title: E. Robust Fusion and Adaptive Weighting
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses a localization system called SLPF, which combines various components such as semantic wall constraints, adaptive GNSS weighting, and robust fusion to improve pose estimation in a vineyard environment. The system is evaluated against other methods, including AMCL, AMCL+NoisyGNSS, and RTAB-Map, in terms of absolute pose error, relative pose error, cross-track error, and row correctness. The results show that SLPF outperforms the other methods, achieving lower absolute pose error and higher row correctness, and demonstrating its ability to mitigate geometric aliasing and recover from wrong-row hypotheses. The document also discusses the experimental setup, including the use of a Thorvald robot platform, a front-mounted Intel RealSense D435i RGB-D camera, a 2D LiDAR, and an RTK-GNSS receiver, and the degradation of RTK trajectory using a stochastic noise model to emulate realistic noisy GNSS conditions.

nodes.title: F. Resampling and pose estimation
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the results of an experiment comparing the performance of different localization methods, including SLPF, AMCL, AMCL+NoisyGNSS, RGB RTAB-Map, and RGBD RTAB-Map. The experiment evaluates the methods' ability to accurately estimate the position and orientation of a robot in a vineyard environment. The results show that SLPF achieves the lowest absolute pose error (APE) among all methods, with an improvement of 22% and 65% over AMCL in Experiment 1 and 2, respectively. The document also presents a failure mode analysis, which highlights the limitations of geometry-only filters in mitigating row-level aliasing, and demonstrates how SLPF addresses this issue through structural semantic modeling and adaptive GNSS weighting. Additionally, the document reports on the robustness of SLPF to detection dropout and map degradation, as well as an ablation study to evaluate the contribution of different components to the overall performance of SLPF. Overall, the results suggest that SLPF provides improved global consistency and corridor adherence across traversal directions, making it a suitable method for localization in vineyard environments.

nodes.title: G. Computational Considerations
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the performance of a localization system called SLPF in an agricultural setting. The main points covered include the comparison of SLPF with other methods, such as AMCL+NoisyGNSS, in terms of row correctness, cross-track error, and failure modes. The document highlights the ability of SLPF to recover from row-mislocalisation events and its robustness to detection dropout and map degradation. The results show that SLPF provides improved global consistency and corridor adherence, and its performance remains stable under increasing semantic sparsity. An ablation study is also presented, which demonstrates the importance of the corridor term and semantic likelihood integration in the SLPF system. Overall, the document suggests that SLPF is a reliable and efficient localization system for agricultural applications.

nodes.title: IV. EXPERIMENTAL EVALUATION
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses a localization system, specifically the Semantic Localisation and Mapping (SLP) framework, which combines various components such as semantic sensing, GNSS prior, corridor prior, and robust fusion to estimate the pose of a robot in an environment. The document covers the mathematical formulation of these components, including the likelihood functions, robust normalization, and adaptive weighting. It also describes the resampling and pose estimation process, computational considerations, and experimental evaluation of the SLP framework in a controlled vineyard environment. The evaluation compares the performance of SLP against other localization methods, including NoisyGNSS, AMCL, and RTAB-Map, using various metrics such as Absolute Pose Error, Relative Pose Error, and cross-track error.

nodes.title: A. Experimental Setup
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses a localization system called SLPF, which combines various components such as semantic wall constraints, adaptive GNSS weighting, and robust fusion to improve pose estimation in a vineyard environment. The system is evaluated against other methods, including AMCL, AMCL+NoisyGNSS, and RTAB-Map, in terms of absolute pose error, relative pose error, cross-track error, and row correctness. The results show that SLPF outperforms the other methods, achieving lower absolute pose error and higher row correctness, and demonstrating its ability to mitigate geometric aliasing and recover from wrong-row hypotheses. The document also discusses the experimental setup, including the use of a Thorvald robot platform, a front-mounted Intel RealSense D435i RGB-D camera, a 2D LiDAR, and an RTK-GNSS receiver, and the degradation of RTK trajectory using a stochastic noise model to emulate realistic noisy GNSS conditions.

nodes.title: B. Results and Discussion
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the results of experiments comparing the performance of different localization methods, including SLPF, AMCL, AMCL+NoisyGNSS, RGB RTAB-Map, and RGBD RTAB-Map. The main points covered include the comparison of absolute pose error (APE) and relative pose error (RPE) across different methods, the evaluation of row-level metrics such as row-correct fraction and cross-track error, and the qualitative analysis of trajectory plots. The document highlights the strengths and weaknesses of each method, with SLPF consistently achieving the lowest APE and highest row-correct fraction. The results suggest that SLPF outperforms other methods in terms of localization accuracy and robustness, particularly in mitigating geometric aliasing and recovering from wrong-row hypotheses.

nodes.title: B. Results and Discussion
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the results of experiments comparing the performance of different localization methods, including SLPF, AMCL, AMCL+NoisyGNSS, RGB RTAB-Map, and RGBD RTAB-Map. The main points covered include the comparison of absolute pose error (APE) and relative pose error (RPE) across different methods, the evaluation of row-level metrics such as row-correct fraction and cross-track error, and the qualitative analysis of trajectory plots. The document highlights the strengths and weaknesses of each method, with SLPF consistently achieving the lowest APE and highest row-correct fraction. The results suggest that SLPF outperforms other methods in terms of localization accuracy and robustness, particularly in mitigating geometric aliasing and recovering from wrong-row hypotheses.

nodes.title: Across both traversal directions, SLPF consistently achieves the lowest raw APE among all methods
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the results of an experiment comparing the performance of different localization methods, including SLPF, AMCL, AMCL+NoisyGNSS, RGB RTAB-Map, and RGBD RTAB-Map. The experiment evaluates the methods' ability to accurately localize a robot in a vineyard environment, with a focus on row-level localization. The results show that SLPF achieves the lowest raw Absolute Pose Error (APE) among all methods, with an improvement of 22% and 65% relative to AMCL and NoisyGNSS, respectively. The document also presents a failure mode analysis, which highlights the limitations of geometry-only filters in mitigating row-level aliasing, and demonstrates how SLPF addresses this issue through structural discrimination and adaptive GNSS weighting. Additionally, the document reports on the robustness of SLPF to detection dropout and map degradation, as well as an ablation study to evaluate the contribution of different components to the overall performance of SLPF.

nodes.title: C. Robustness to Detection and Map Degradation
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the performance of a localization system called SLPF in an agricultural setting. The main points covered include the comparison of SLPF with other methods, such as AMCL+NoisyGNSS, in terms of row correctness, cross-track error, and failure modes. The document also presents the results of experiments, including a failure mode analysis, performance analysis, and robustness tests under detection dropout and map degradation. Additionally, an ablation study is reported to evaluate the contribution of different components of the SLPF system. The results suggest that SLPF provides improved global consistency and corridor adherence, and is robust to increased perceptual ambiguity and moderate landmark loss. The system's performance is also evaluated in terms of runtime, with the full SLPF pipeline running at approximately 13 Hz. Overall, the document presents a detailed analysis of the SLPF system's performance and robustness in an agricultural localization task.

nodes.title: We evaluate two stress tests on Experiment 1
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the Semantic Landmark Particle Filter (SLPF) method for robust localization in vineyards. The main points covered include the comparison of SLPF with other methods such as AMCL and NoisyGNSS, the analysis of failure modes and performance, and the evaluation of the method's robustness to detection and map degradation. The document also presents an ablation study to analyze the contribution of different components of the SLPF method, including semantic walls, poles, trunks, GNSS prior, pose smoothing, background, and corridor terms. The results show that SLPF improves global map-frame accuracy and corridor adherence, and reduces absolute pose error and cross-track error. The method's limitations and potential future work are also discussed, including dynamic map updating, cross-season adaptation, and extension to other structured agricultural environments.

nodes.title: D. Ablation Study
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the results of an ablation study for a Semantic Landmark Particle Filter (SLPF) used for robust localization in vineyards. The study evaluates the impact of various components, including semantic walls, poles and trunks, GNSS prior, pose smoothing, background and corridor terms, and semantic likelihood, on the performance of the SLPF. The results show that combining semantic classes, using dynamic weighting for GNSS prior, and incorporating background and corridor terms improve the stability and accuracy of the localization. The study also highlights the importance of semantic information and GNSS prior in achieving robust localization in repetitive environments. The document concludes by discussing the limitations of the proposed formulation and outlining future work directions, including cross-seasonal robustness and extension to other structured agricultural environments.

The document also discusses the limitations of the method and potential future work, including dynamic map updating, cross-season adaptation, and extension to other structured agricultural environments.

nodes.title: V. CONCLUSION
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the results of an ablation study on the Semantic Landmark Particle Filter (SLPF) method for robust localization in vineyards. The study evaluates the impact of various components, including semantic walls, GNSS prior, pose smoothing, background and corridor terms, and semantic likelihood cues, on the performance of the SLPF method. The results show that combining semantic classes, using dynamic weighting for GNSS prior, and incorporating structural regularizers such as background and corridor terms improve the stability and accuracy of the method. The study also highlights the importance of semantic information and GNSS prior in achieving robust localization in repetitive environments. Additionally, the document discusses the limitations of the proposed formulation and outlines future work directions, including cross-seasonal robustness and extension to other structured agricultural environments.

nodes.title: ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS
nodes.summary: The partial document appears to be a section of a research paper focused on autonomous navigation in agricultural environments, particularly in vineyards and orchards. The main points covered include the use of generative AI tools in the preparation of the manuscript, and a comprehensive list of references citing various studies on topics such as lidar-based structure tracking, vision and 2D lidar fusion-based navigation, loop closure detection, and semantic slam systems. The references also touch on the use of machine learning algorithms, such as neural networks, for slam/gnss fusion localization and the application of RTK GPS and Pixhawk for autonomous navigation. Additionally, the document mentions the use of datasets, such as the Bacchus Long-Term (BLT) data set, and libraries like RTAB-Map for large-scale and long-term online operation. Overall, the partial document suggests that the research paper is exploring the application of advanced navigation systems and machine learning techniques in agricultural robotics.
